params_history.analysis: Overall win rate is 27.8% (15/54 decided trades) with a strong directional bias: SELL trades won 14/25 (56%) while BUY trades won only 1/29 (3.4%). The market exhibited a persistent bearish trend across this 10-day period, making demand zones (BUY) consistently fail. Symbols like RELIANCE (6/6 SELL wins), ICICIBANK, and BHARTIARTL performed well on SELL side, while TCS BUY trades lost all 5 attempts totaling ₹-210.

With a 22.6% win rate (12/53 decided trades) and heavy losses totaling ₹-756, the current parameters are far too permissive. BUY trades were catastrophic with only 5 wins vs ~35 losses, particularly BHARTIARTL.NS (0 wins, 8+ losses losing ₹-335) and TCS.NS BUY (0 wins, 6 losses). SELL trades performed relatively better at 7 wins from fewer attempts, suggesting a bearish market regime where demand zones are consistently failing.

Win rate is 37% (20/72) with a negative P&L of ₹-250. SELL trades significantly outperform BUY trades with 13 wins vs 7 wins despite fewer total sell trades, suggesting a bearish market bias during this period. BUY trades on TCS.NS (-36 avg loss), INFY.NS (-24 avg loss), RELIANCE.NS, and BHARTIARTL.NS are consistently hitting stop losses, indicating demand zones are failing to hold.

With only 4 wins out of 83 trades (9.5% win rate, ₹-666 P&L), the system is catastrophically over-trading with extremely poor zone selection. 38 SL_HIT losses indicate zones are being entered against the prevailing trend — 70+ of 83 trades were SELL-side during what appears to be a broadly bullish/choppy market. EXPIRED trades (41 of 83) with small negative P&L suggest zones are being touched but price lacks momentum to reach targets at 2.0 RR.

Win rate of 18.4% (9/70) is catastrophically low, with only 9 TARGET_HITs against 40 SL_HITs and 21 EXPIRED trades. TCS.NS is the worst performer with 9 losses and only 1 win (P&L approx -₹281), while BHARTIARTL.NS shows 0 wins from 10 trades. The average winning trade (₹21.1) vs average SL loss (₹-18.5) shows the 2.0 RR ratio is not being achieved in practice, suggesting zones are too close to price action or stop-losses are too tight.

final_summary.executive_summary: The walk-forward simulation of 454 trades over 12 weeks produced a dismal 25.4% overall win rate with consistent negative P&L, indicating the zone scanner alone is insufficient for profitable trading. The system chronically over-traded (30-44 trades/week) with poor zone quality filtering, frequently entering against prevailing trends. The only profitable week (Week 5, 50% WR, ₹+35) coincided with higher min_score thresholds and reduced RR targets, while catastrophic weeks (4 and 7 at 7.7% and 4.0% WR) occurred when the system traded aggressively into trending markets without directional bias filtering.
key_insights: Zone scoring threshold of 70 is far too permissive - the system generates excessive low-quality signals that get stopped out; weeks with implied higher filtering (Week 5 at 50% WR, Week 12 at 42.9% WR) dramatically outperformed, suggesting min_score of 82-85 is necessary to filter noise

The 2.0 RR ratio is rarely achieved in practice - actual winning trades averaged only ₹21.1 while stops averaged ₹18.5 (effective RR ~1.14:1), meaning price enters zones but lacks momentum to reach full targets; a 1.3-1.5 RR with higher win rate is mathematically superior for this setup

Trading against the prevailing trend is the #1 killer - SELL zones in bullish markets and BUY zones in bearish markets account for the majority of SL_HIT losses; TCS.NS (9 losses/1 win) and BHARTIARTL.NS (0 wins/10 trades) show certain instruments need directional bias confirmation before entry

EXPIRED trades (averaging 40-50% of all trades) with small negative P&L indicate zones are being touched but price consolidates rather than reversing sharply - tighter base candle requirements (max 2-3) would select only the sharpest departure zones with genuine institutional interest

The optimizer consistently reverted to permissive params (min_score 70, RR 2.0) because it lacked enough winning trade data to validate tighter settings - this is a classic overfitting-to-noise trap where the system needs fewer, higher-conviction trades rather than more attempts
final_summary.best_performing_setup: Week 5 (50% WR, only profitable week) and Week 12 (42.9% WR, smallest loss) shared common characteristics: fewer total trades (31-32 vs 40+ average), implying natural market conditions filtered out weak zones. The Claude synthesis recommendations of min_score 82, RR 1.5, max_base_candles 3 were directionally correct but never given sufficient runway to prove themselves as the mini_optimizer kept reverting to looser params. The strongest pattern appears to be fresh (untested) zones with very tight bases (2-3 candles), high departure momentum, entered only in alignment with the higher-timeframe trend direction.
final_summary.recommended_live_approach: Drastically reduce trade frequency by implementing strict quality gates: minimum zone score of 85, maximum 2-3 base candles for sharp institutional prints, RR target of 1.5 to improve hit rate, and critically add a trend-alignment filter (only take BUY zones in uptrending instruments, SELL zones in downtrending). Limit to 3-5 trades per day maximum across all instruments. Avoid TCS.NS and BHARTIARTL.NS unless zone scores exceed 90. Consider partial profit-taking at 1.0 RR with trail to 1.5 RR to capture the frequent 'touch-and-fade' behavior observed in expired trades. The zone scanner should be treated as a candidate generator, not a trade signal - additional confluence (volume spike at zone, higher-timeframe alignment, time-of-day filter avoiding first 15 and last 15 minutes) is essential for profitability.
